serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beef as a main dish component in Singapore is approximately 150g, equivalent to a palm-sized portion of cooked beef.

nutrient_source.title: Beef (牛肉) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef (牛肉), cr_id G00011062, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select vitamins not reported in HPB entry; set to -1 where unavailable. USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 13365, Beef, ground, 85% lean / 15% fat, cooked) used as secondary reference to cross-validate macro plausibility.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. Pure cooked beef and beef dishes with negligible net carbohydrate content have an effective GI of 0 as established by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database, which assigns GI=0 to pure protein foods with negligible carbohydrate. Although carbohydrate is 4.19g/100g (primarily from marinade), the effective glycaemic response is negligible. GI is set to 0 (numeric) and gi_category to 'low'. GL per 150g serving = (0 × 4.19 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 369mg/100g is borderline-high; partially offset by potassium at 368mg/100g (Na:K ratio ~1:1)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in preparation to lower sodium below 300mg/100g, and choose lean beef cuts to minimise saturated fat intake, as the near 1:1 sodium-to-potassium ratio provides partial but not complete cardiovascular protection.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (18.48g/100g) slows gastric emptying; carbohydrate (4.19g/100g) and sugar (1.45g/100g) are very low, supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (4.19g/100g) and high protein (18.48g/100g) which attenuates postprandial glucose response; however, lean cuts should be selected to minimise saturated fat, which can worsen insulin resistance, and low-sodium soy sauce should be used in marinades.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 52mg/100g and unquantified saturated fat content in cooked beef preparation; red meat consumption is associated with increased CVD risk at high intake levels
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit beef consumption to moderate portions (≤150g per serving, ≤3 times per week), select lean cuts to minimise saturated fat, and balance intake with vegetables and whole grains to reduce overall dietary cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit beef consumption as its arachidonic acid content promotes pro-inflammatory eicosanoid synthesis; if consumed, pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as turmeric or oily fish and limit to small infrequent portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef (牛肉) is a cooked beef dish commonly served in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and home kitchens, typically prepared by stir-frying or braising beef cuts with soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and aromatics. The dish delivers a savoury, umami-rich flavour with a tender to slightly chewy texture depending on the cut and cooking duration. Nutritionally, it provides 148 kcal per 100g with a high protein content of 18.48g, moderate fat at 6.0g, and low carbohydrate at 4.19g, making it a protein-dense, low-glycaemic food suitable for most dietary patterns. Sodium is borderline at 369mg/100g due to soy sauce marinade, partially offset by potassium at 368mg/100g. Iron content at 1.84mg/100g contributes meaningfully to daily iron requirements. Clinically, beef is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load and high protein, but should be consumed in moderation by those with hypertension (due to sodium), cardiovascular disease (due to cholesterol and saturated fat), and avoided or minimised by those with rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid content. As a Singaporean mixed dish, it is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is a staple protein source across Chinese, Malay, and Indian-influenced cooking traditions in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in beef?
Can I eat beef if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of beef?
Is beef suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?
